PREFACE

Tais is a book for the Gaels of Ireland. They will understand
it, and if it pleases them, T do not care whether anyone else
in the world values or condemns it!

TLong ago the writer learned from the hurlers the secret
of the game—to be swift to strike when the chance came—
never to quail, but with tightened lips to bore in against the
fiercest and most overwhelming onslaughts. When victorious,
to be kindly and modest, when bruised and beaten, cheerfully
to make the best of it! Not bad rules these, even for the
more serious conflict of Life.

The Gaels, unrivalled athletes, taught also, by example,
what real manliness is. No cursing, swearing, or obscenity
was tolerated in their midst. They were pure in thought and
act, they feared God and loved His Church. Ideal companions
they were, friends whose love was “ passing the love of woman.”

If T were engaged in a struggle for life, surrounded by
the most fearful dangers, I could not ask for more stalwart
hands or truer hearts than theirs to rush to my aid in the
Bearna Baoghail ; and if it were mine to choose the hands
that at the last would lower my body into its house of clay, I
would choose the strong, honest, tender hands of the Gaels
of Ireland! Grasa Dhe chughainn, agus bas ¢ n-Eirinn!

DerMoT ROCHE, the stalwart young captain of the Moondharrig
Hurlers, was ploughing that portion of his farm known as
Gurtharda, or the High Fields. It was an event important
enough in Moondharrig. Gurtharda, that ideal sporting
ground, dry and level, and several acres in extent, where the
hurlers had mustered so many a happy day; Gurtharda, that
had so often rung to the shout of the Gaels and the clash of
the whirling caman; its glory would soon be a thing of the
past. In all the parish there was not one but was sorry to
see the beautiful green sward overturned.

The Captain had made some demur at first when ordered
to “break ” Gurtharda; but “Himself,”—Dermot’s father, a
stern and practical old farmer, had determined onit, and would
brook no opposition.

“The fields are goin’ to ruin as it is,” said “ Himself;”
“they’ll be good for nothing but growing noonyeens an’ bowchi-
laun-buidhe if they’re not tilled this year. Besides, you an’
your hurlers have the land cut to pieces, an’ ye’ll have to find
some other practice-ground.”

“Well, there is Gurth-Branair the fallow-field, sir,” put

'in Dermot respectfully, “if you'd let us practice in that, it ‘ud

do first-rate.”

“ Aye, that’d do, but it wouldn’t answer,” rejoined * Him-
self ” testily. “It’s my fields ye're after all the time, I notice ;
why don’t ye try some o’ the neighbours now an’ then ?”

“Lots o’ them 'ud be proud to give a field if we asked
’em, but they’re poor men, an’ the boys don’t want to be too
hard on ’em.” “I suppose,” added the Captain, “ we’ll have
to change the practice-ground to the other side o’ the parish.
Na-Killya, from the Glens, wants us to take his big field,
‘Kyleavorouan,” down by the river.”

Now, Captain Dermot was playing a pretty sly game, and

As the Captain sang, Pete O’Hara, the erack seriob (for-
ward) of the Moondharrig Hurlers, came along. He wasa
lithe, active youth, and was dressed in a new black corduroy
trousers, a flannel waistcoat as white as snow folded softly
about his body, and & slouch hat pulled down a little over his
eyes.
“(od save you, Pete,” saluted the Captain.

“God save you kindly, an’ bless the work,” answered
O’Hara, “though it’s sorry 1am,” he continued, “to see the
work you're at. What'll we do now, at all, for a field to
practice in,” and the scriob-mam’s voice was as sad as though
something grievous had befallen him.

«1ts all right, Pete,” said the Captain. ‘Himself’ said
he'd let us have Gurth-Branair for the season.”

«1id he say that,” cried O’Hara joyously. « That's great
news. Machree wm cleabh thu ! 1 know 'twas yourself got it out
of him. We'd have no business at all facin’ Tipperary without
lots o’ practice. They’d make a show of us. Did you hea
from ’em lately about the match ?”

«Yes. I had a letter from the Moyecullen G.A.A. secre-
tary. He says they play the Metropolitans in Dublin next
Friday, and will meet usin Clonmel three weeks from to-day,
so we must practice every evening from this on. I must send
word down to the Glen Boys to-night” added the Captain.

«T hear Martin Quinn is goin’ to America,” remarked
O'Hara. “ What!” exclaimed the Captain, his face clouding.
« Martin going, ’an he one of our best defence men. Will they

never stop goin’ to America,” he continued bitterly. “There’s

10 use tryin’ to keep up a team at this rate. You remember
the year we won the All-Ireland Championship—ten o’ the
players went in a month, an’ never came back. I wouldn’t
mind so muchif the Angishores went, but ’tis always the best
men. An’ what do they get for it?” he went on angrily.
¢ Tots o’ hard work and an early death—I never saw oneo’
them come back yet that was good for anything. They're old
men before their time.

THE broad, well-kept public road running from

City to Clonmel, through the Counties of Kgilkennyxz::ger'ﬁn%
perary, traverses as fine a pastoral country as can be seen anP-
where in th.e world. Like a great, white ribbon the road wings
across the rich, green fields of the Suir Valley and the Golden
Vale, keeping the river a companion the whole length of its
course. Clusters of villages, and the large, stone-built, slate-
roofed houses of “strong-farmers,” stand along its bord’er '

On the day of which we speak, tho dust of this fine high-
way was stirred by the passing of a British Cavalry Regiment
from Waterford to the Military Barracks at Clonmel. The
Tenth Lincolnshire Hussars, for such was the name‘ of the
regiment, though English in title, had, as is almost always
the case, very many Irish and Scotchmen in its make-up.
The Huss‘ars made a fine and spirited spectacle with theix'-
showy uniforms and well-caparisoned horses, though, the day
being warm, they were dusty and perspiring from t-in; march

The Tenth was a veteran regiment, having seen muc.h
service in Egypt and Northern Hindostan ; in fact, it had but
lately landed from these foreign shores. ;

Two officers at the head of the regiment were conversing
earngstly. One was the commander, Colonel Dinsmore, an
Englishman born. He was of short, stocky build, and his hair
and moustache were grizzled with long service. His com-
panion was an Irishman, a Lieutenant, by the name of Burke.
This officer was still quite a young man, tall and lithe of body.
and of the type that fights for the sake of fighting in no rmattex"
what cause.

8 
THE GAELS OF MOONDHARRIG. 
"This is really a fine country, Lieutenant," the Colonel 
was saying, "a veritable Paradise after the red sands of the 
Soudan. Many a hot day, on the trail of the Mahdi, I longed 
for these things: the green fields, trees bending with leaves, 
cool, clear streams and rivers." 
"It is, indeed, a fine country," answered Burke, with some 
show of pride, "they raise the right kind of men around here, 
too ;-too bad we can't get them for the army," he a~ded •. 
"We don't recruit very many men here, then? 
queried 
the Colonel. 
"Hardly a baker's dozen," rejoined his companion. "We 
get a few of the day-labourer's sons, but never one of the yo'-:ng 
farmers-the strono- active fellows who make up the hurlmg 
teams. 
These latt:r have a hatred for the army, and if they 
cannot live at home., contrive to get to America some.how. 
After all, Colonel, we get but the riff-raff of Ireland in the 
army, and yet how splendidly these poor fellows fight. 
By 
thunder, if we could get together a regiment of the hmlers, 
they'd go though the enemy like fire through dry gorse ! We 
had a few hurlers in the Royal Rifles, and when we came to 
close quarters, I noticed them club muskets, left hand u~der-
neath, in hurling fashion, and they bored through the tribes-
men as cheerfully as ducks through the rain." 
"You don't say so," said the Colonel, smiling at his com-
panion's impetuosity. " I have heard it spoken of as a game 
that makes men and fighters, and I believe you're right. We'll 
never have the proper training in the army until we adopt 
some manly game like that, and keep otir men from becoming 
the pipeclay dudes they so often turn out to be. 
By the way," 
added the Colonel, " do those hurlers of yours practice 
wrestling ? " 
'' They have no superiors at it," asserted Burke with 
earnestness. " When a game is over the crowds form a ring, and 
the champion wrestlers of the home-team, 1·eady-stripped, 
stand inside and challenge their opponents. 
They usually 
don't have long to wait, and I've seen four pairs struggling in 
the ring at once." 
"Are they really skilful at it, though. 
You know we 
have Akenside, a sergeant here in the regiment, who was the 
THE WRESTLING AT GURTHARDA. 
9 
best professional wrestler in England a few years ago, and I 
believe he's just as good to-day. 
"I warrant you, Colonel," replied the Lieutenant "that 
the pick of these Gaelic athletes is a match tor any professional 
-fail- and square rules being observed." 
"You surprise me, and I can hardly believe it. 
By the 
way, we should be near to that spring of water we were informed 
of. 
I believe this is it, and a glorious spring-well it is, too. 
Order a halt, we shall feed and water the horses here." 
The 1·egiment halted with a great dash of hoofs and sword-
sheaths, and the men dismounting proceeded to attend to the 
wants of their horses. 
While the latter were feeding, the hussars gathered in 
groups, and proceeded to have some fun on their own account. 
A red-headed,jolly-faced "Corkonian," named Billy Hannigan, 
seemed to be the centre of attraction, and around ·him the 
largest crowd gathered; Englishmen for the most part. 
"Come, Billy, old boy, give us a Come-all-ye,-wo 'aven't 
'eard your voice for an age." But Billy modestly declined on 
account of an alleged hoarseness. 
" Besides, boys, the day ii,

Wiid applause broke forth at the end of Billy's song. 
He 
was evidently, in spite of his rebellious spirit, a great favourite 
with the troopers, and had doubtless helped to brighten many 
a tedious hour ere this. 
"Let us have the bloomin' ditty hover again, Billy," cried 
Cockney Joe enthusiastically. 
"Nao bog atha 'she agath ! (isn't it soft you have it) 
you spriss aun o' the world, an' it such a dry day, too," retorted 
the singer, dropping for a moment into his native Gaelic. 
The Colonel and Lieutenant Burke were standing a short 
distance away, enjoying the whole proceedings. 
"I can't understand the Irish troops," remarked the 
Colonel, "they fight so well in battle, and out of battle talk 
utter treason, like our friend, Billy here. 
Wasn't this man 
mentioned for bravery on some occr,sion?" 
"He got a medal for saving his officer at Tel-el-K.ebir, 
and received two spear wounds at Taman-i-beb. He is one of 
the bra vest fellows I ever knew. 
We can't blame our Irish 
soldiers for their poor sense of loyalty. 
Many of them have 
seen their parents turned out of their homes by the same 
British army, during eviction raids." 
"That's a poor state of affairs, I must admit," said the 
Colonel, " with the army so badly in need of such men just 
now. 
For my own part, I have always favoured a Home Rule 
Scheme, and I still believe old Gladstone should have been 
given a free hand. He knew more than all our puny states-
men together. 
By the way, I have set my mind on pitting 
Akenside against some of your hurlers here. 
I see a tall 
fellow ploughing in that field close by-suppose you inquire 
if there are any good wrestlers around."

12 THE GAELS OF MOONDHARRIG.

It happened that the regiment had halted near Gurtharda,
and Dermot Roche pulled up his horses as he saw the young
officer approaching.

“God bless the work,” said the Lieutenant, saluting in
the Irish manner, and the young farmer answered courteously,
“God save you, sir.”

The Lieutenant came to the point at once. “We have a
professional English wrestler in the troop, and I wish you could
get some of the hurlers to try him. Is the Captain of the team
anywhere around ?” “Yes, sir; he is,” said Dermot Roche, a
little proudly. “Heis right here befors you.”

“This is a great pleasure, I'm sure,” said the soldier.
“You're just the man I want. You weren’t made Captain
without deserving it—what do you say to trying the English-
man yourself?”

“I haven’t any objection in the world,” answered the Cap-
tain readily; “it will be a welcome change after the
ploughin’.”

Lieutenant Burke looked at the bronzed face of the young
farmer, then noted his broad, herculean span of shoulders,
and his powerful, symmetrical limbs. As he went back to the
road he muttered :—* This is about the Iuckiest thing in the
world. T expect I'll surprise the old man a little.” Climbing
over the fence he saluted and announced—¢The young lad in
the field, sir, said he would like to try Akenside himself.”

“What!” exclaimed the Colonel; “he did, did he? Have
you told him that our man is a famous professional.”

“He didn’t seem tobe at all bothered by that fact, sir.”

“ Well, well, well,” ejaculated the old soldier, “this is a
surprising country, indeed—a most surprising country ! ”

The young ploughman in the meantime had brought his
horses to the “headland,” and turned them loose to graze.

Akenside was called, and the officers and soldiers entered
Gurtharda. Roche had divested himself of his flannel waist-
coat,and in his white shirt made a splendid and spirited figureas
he stood awaiting them—a typical representative of “the finest
peasantry in the world,” thought Lieutenant Burke. Akenside
now stripped for the bout. He was not so tall as the young
peasant by about three inches, but seemed ¢ stockier” and of

THE WRESTLING AT GURTHARDA. 13

heavier build—in fact, he seemed to the Lieutenant to be too
fleshy for the work in hand. His skin-tight riding-suit showed
off his well-developed limbs to great advantage, and made
him look a most formidable adversary.

The halting of the military had been observed from afar,
and from all sides the people hastened to view the proceedings.
They watched the preparations for the contest with breathless
interest.

Dermot Roche knew without looking in their faces that
their hearts and souls were with him, and that if he failed it
would be looked at almost in the light of a National calamity.
1t was the old story over again of the Saxonand the Gael, and
this time there was a fair field and no favour.

The two wrestlers shook hands and took hold.

“God strengthen your arm, Dermot,” said one of the crowd
fervently. :

«“ Hakenside, me lad, ’eave into the bloomin’ Irishman,”
said Cockney Joe.

From the first the professional wrestler seemed to think
he had an easy proposition on hand. He rushed things at
once, but gradually and with amazement had to acknowledge
that his opponent was practised and skilful. His rapid suc-
cession of feints and tricks were met and frustrated with
apparent ease, though, at the same time, he got no measure of
the real strength and force of the Captain of the hurlers.

As the time went by, and the wrestlers wheeled round and
round in their strugle, it was evident to the onlookers that the
Englishman was becoming puffed and played out, while Roche,
so well used to hard work and swift running, seemed almost
as fresh as when he started. It had been declared beforehand
that whoever took the first two falls out of three bouts would
be the winner, and the people began to have hopes that their
champion would be able to take the first. “ Manam asthee hu ;
Dermot! Keep at him, boy. The Sassenach is weakening,”
cried Malachy Gilmartin, one of the Glen boys, who happened
to be passing. “Did you try the Kilmockler ‘trascairt’ on

him yet ?”
This term must be explained. There was a district a half-
dozen miles to the north of Moondharrig, called Kilmockler,

14 
THE GAELS OF MOONDHARRIG. 
noted for its famous wrestlers. 
The Captain had spent many 
a day there getting initiated into all the mysteries of the 
manly exercise. 
The trascairt, or " overthrow," was a trick, quick 
and irresistible, of putting down an adversary, the secret of 
which they revealed only to their most brilliant pupils. 
The Captain had been altogether passive and on the 
defensive up to this, but now he changed his tactics. 
In a 
moment the soldier found himself violently whirled, and shaken 
up; the great, long arms of the hurler gradually contracting 
and drawing him close. 
A tremendous pressure was employed 
to force him in a certain direction, then, in a flash, he was en-
dangered from another side. At last the knee of the young 
ploughman was locked into that of Akenside like a steel bar. 
It was a case of dislocated bones, or of falling, and the English-
man fell very unwillingly. It was first fall for the Captain, 
and while the people shouted, the soldiers looked at one 
another in wonde1·. 
"I'll look out for that the next time," said Akenside with 
an ugly smile, as they took hold a second time. 
This bout was not long in progress when there occurred a 
great commotion among the horses on the roadway, and the 
crowd turned from the contest to see what the matter was. 
Young Roche also, very foolishly, suffered himself to be 
distracted from his work, and relaxed his guard. The trained 
professional profited by this on the instant. 
Going low, he 
rushed in and overthrew his opponent falling heavily on him. 
It was rather comical to notice the consternation on the 
faces of the crowd when, as they turned about, they saw their 
champion on the ground. 
_ 
The English soldiers cheered lustily, and things looked 
rather dark for the Captain. Many an anxious prayer went 
up from the people that he might not fail in the deciding trial, 
and his cheerful and confident face, as he stood up again, went 
far to reassure their drooping hearts. He shook ha1i.ds man-
fully with the soldier. 
" You did that nicely, sir," he said, 
"And I deserved all I got." 
Then the men settled down to the deciding tussle. The 
stillness that fell on the spectators now was intense-so acute 
THE WRESTLING AT GURTHARDA. 
15 
was their interest in the struggle. A jaunting car drove up, 
unobserved, and a very tall, elderly man entered the field and 
began to look on with the others. 
This deciding contest was 
longer than the previous ones. Each of th_e men endeavoured 
to exhaust his opponent before attempting a decisive venture. 
It was evident, however, that the Englishman would once more 
become played out before young Roche showed any signs 
of fatigue. 
The face of the former was flushed and perspiring, 
and his breathing became laboured, as time went by, while the 
Captain's face kept its cool, determined look, his teeth being 
shut like a vice, and his breathing scarcely audible. Among 
his most ardent backers were the Irish soldiers, headed by 
Trooper Hannigan. 
"It's all up with Akenside," whispered the latter to his 
comrades. "In a minnit he'll try one of his foul turns-the 
trick he nearly always plays-see if he don't." 
Then Trooper Hannigan's voice broke the utter silence. 
He spoke in low, mellow, rapid Gaelic, and the Captain started 
perceptibly as he heard. 
When the gyration of the struggle 
brought them in line, bis eyes caught Hannigan's for a fraction 
of a second. 
"Ar m'anam, go bh-fuil an cearth agath, a mhic'' (on 
my soul you're right, my son) he answered. 
The words were scarcely spoken when the professional's 
hand was seen moving in the direction of the Captain's throat. 
An angry flash came into the eyes of the latter. 
The 
hand was struck down in an instant. Then the Captain shifted 
the grip of both his hands, the force of a half dozen men 
seemed to come into his arms, and the body of the English-
man crumpled up in that terrible hug. 
With a superb exhi-
bition of strength the young Gael drew his opponent in and up 
0ver his shoulder. Here he appeared to hold him for a breath-
less moment; then he leaped from his feet and down they 
came with a sickening thud, the soldier underneath. It was the 
winning fall ! 
The thing looked easy and simple-as a mrttter of fact it 
• was a complex enough feat, and irresistible to the uninitiated. 
It was, in truth the famous Kilmockler trascairt that Dermot 
had employed to the complete discomfiture of Akenside.

THE GAELS OF MOONDHARRIG. 
The joy of the people knew no bounds at seeing the 
triumph of their hero. Tears of pride stood on the cheeks of 
several. 
The men rushed in and caught his hands, almost 
hugging him in their delight and affection, and foremost -
amongst them was Trooper Hannigan. 
"My dear boy," said 
the trooper, • "you did us the best turn you ever did, to stop 
that fellow's boasting. 
He was for ever shouting what he 
could do to an Irishman at the wrestling." 
" Well, I'm glad 
you're pleased," answered the Captain. "You gave me a good 
warning in the ring. 
You're too much of a fine fellow to be 
wearing the red jacket, an' I'm sorry to see it on you-Now be 
aisy, boys," he cried, addressing the crowd, "sure it's not 
such a great thing I did. 
I mightn't do as well if he had me 
over in England. 
Every dog is bold in his own door-way, an' 
I'm afraid the poor fellow is hurt." 
The Colonel and the Lieutenant were examining Aken-
side's arm and shoulder as the Captain went towards him. 
"I'm sorry, sir," he declared simply, "that you got hurt. I'd 
never ask to meet a better man. 
Come here, Denis Maher, 
it's lucky you're around. 
He won't do you the least harm, sir, 
an' he'll set your arm in a jiffy." 
Denis Maher was the Moondharrig "bonesetter.'' 
The 
Gaels declared him "better than the best o' the doctors,'' and 
gave him frequent opportunity to keep his hand in practice. 
He felt the injured shoulder an instant with deft fingers, 
then with a dexterous jerk snapped the dislocated bone into 
position. With a couple of knotted handkerchiefs he impro-
vised an arm-sling. " You're as right as a fiddle now, sir," said 
he, "we're used to these little things among the hurlers." 
The grizzled Colonel shook hands warmly with Captain 
Dermot Roche. 
" I only wish I commanded a regiment of 
your stamp," he said. 
Then he gave a sharp order, and the 
soldiers left the field. Dermot Roche put on his flannel waist-
coat, and leaving the people walked quietly towards his 
horses; but the tall man who had come on the jaunting-car 
rushed to him and caught his hand. With a start of surprise 
the Captain recognised O'Dwyer, the farmer, who lived in the 
white-gabled house on the hill-side, and who had driven him 
away so ignominiously on a former occasion.

ing. "I saw you. put down the Sassenach, and I'll never

Come up to the house and see us to-morrow, boy. 
I know 
somebody that'll be delighted to see you."

When Night is on the Suir. 
I. 
ALONG towards evening of the day of the big wrestling-match, 
M.eehal-na-Gamawn came along to have an interview with his 
friend the Captain. 
Meehal had received his soubriquet on 
account of his fondness for the national game, and for every-
thing connected with it. 
His admiration for the hurlers 
and particularly for Dermot Roche, amounted almost to 
idolatry. 
He was never absent from any of the games or practice-
matches. In body Meehal was strong and thick-set, and his 
face was always like the rising sun, beaming with good-
nature. Though his mind was shrewd enough in some 
respects, there were certain points in which he was extra-
ordinarily childish and credulous. 
This latter trait of his, 
however, only increased the affection and regard which every 
man of the Gaels held towards him. 
"Well, Meehal," spoke the Captain, smiling-and Det·mot 
Roche's smile was good to see-" what have you got to say fo1· 
yourself now ? " 
"I was down the Glens this mornin' wud a theseaun of 
oats to the mill, an' I was talkin' to some o' the hurlers. They 
want you down to the practice in Shaun-na-Killya's moon-
thaun this evenin'." 
"They do, eh ; what's the matter with the Glen fellows 
now?" queried the Captain. 
"It takes me all my time to 
manage between the Glensmen and the Mountain-men, they're 
so jealous of each other on the team. 
Besides, I was half-
thinkin' o' going up the hills to-night," and the Captain 
frowned at the innocent Meehal, as if that individual were to 
blame.

19 
"Well, 'tisn't my fault, Captain," protested Meehal. 
"You see they held a meetin' down there-J ack-rn1,-Killya, 
Connor O'Hara, Jimmy Wat-Bawn, an' five or six o' the 
W alshes, besides Gilmartin, the W oodr:1nger, an' they want to 
have you put Moran Og O'Loughlin on the team in the place 
of Martin Quinn that's goin' to America. 
They say Moran is 
a grand hurler entirely." 
"Young Moran is a good-enough hurler" assented the 
Captain, "but we have just as good up here, an' they're all 
anxious to get on the team. The Glen boys are not a bit too 
modest-they want everything they see. 
Martin Quinn is a 
man from our side, an' why can't they lot us put one of our 
(,Wll boys in his place." 
" There was another thing they told me be sure an' tell 
you,'' put in Meehal with a grin. 
"There's a few o' the boys 
goin' down the Curraghaun to-night for a scrape, an' they're 
bound to have you go with 'em." 
"Ah! that's it, eh," laughed the Captain, "fishing in the 
'closed season,' against the law, an' if there's to be a mix-up 
with the peelers, they want me to enjoy the fun ! • Well, I 
suppose I must go down an' see the new hurler anyway, what-
ever happens after.'' 
The Captain took out his watch, which, by the way, he 
had won in London at an international heavy-weight throwing 
exhibition. 
" It's near six o'clock,'' he said. '' I'll just finish this sod, 
an' then we'll go to supper. 
We'll be down to the Glens 
together. 
There's nothing like company to shorten the road." 
II. 
THE little village of Ballyshanmore, nestled down in the 
"Glens," close to the River Suir. The houses, some thatch-
roofed, some with roofs of slate, stood close together, as if for 
companionship, against the cruel forces of Landlordism, which 
ever threaten the peace of the Irish tenant-farmer. 
The villagers, in the intervals of farming, were wont to 
do "a stroke of fishing" in the great, broad river flowing 
past, but were not regular fisher-folk by any means. It was

20 THE GAELS OF MOONDHARRIG.

approaching dusk as Dermot Roche and Meehal-na-Gamawn
came up the street and entered one of the houses, whose
windows were already brightly lighted. We will take our
accustomed privilege and enter with them ere they have time
to let the latch drop against us.

The sheen of the fire-light glancing on the five rows ol
burnished plates on the dresser, almost takes the sight of our
eyes from us. The row of well-scrubbed wooden pails with
flashing hoop on the long stillion reaching from Cush-a-
thimmay to the dresser, bespeak the care of the Vamthee and
her buxom daughters. The hard cement floor is clean and
well-swept.

Around a fire of blazing coals a half-dozen of the Glen
hurlers were seated in a circle beneath the great wide chim-
ney. They were tall slashing youths, half-fishers, half-farmers,
and trained to the game from early boyhood.

The entrance of the two Mountain-men caused quite a
hubbub, and the deep-toned exclamations of pleasure and
welcorse showed their great esteem for the Captain.

T heard ye have a young hurler here ye want tried for
Martin Quinn’s place ” began the latter when he had got
seated in their midst.

His words were particularly addressed to Malachy Gil-
martin, the “Woodranger,” a powerfully built young man,
courageous as & lion, who was gamekeeper to a local noble-
man.

“Yes” answered the Woodranger, ¢ that’s true enough,
bus there’s lots o’ time for that yet. There’s to be a spring-
tide to-night on the river, an’ great fishin’; we were thinking
yowd maybe like to try that fixst. It's just a few days into
the *closed season,” and the fish are better than ever, but the
peelers an’ water-bailiffs are keepin’ a sharp eye out. I saw,”
continued Gilmartin, “Head-constable Flynn an’ two o’ the
men from the barracks patrollin’ there to-day, but we'll have
a fish in spite of them. Why should they stop us now an’
leave the gentlemen-anglers, landlords an’ shoneens two
months longer to fish. It's always one law for the poor an’
another for the rich ! ”

“Well,” said Dermot Roche. “T suppose if we're goin’ at

WHEN NIGHT IS ON THE SUIR. 21

all we may as well go now, boys—here’s good-luck to us to-
night, an’ that we may escape the peelers.”

At his word the young men arose and went out in a body.
A short walk through the fields, already wet with the night-
dew, brought them to the river-banks.

Among the long reeds, securely hidden, the boats and
nets were soon found.

Each “crew ” consisted of four men, two to a boat. The
long narrow boats, called “ cots,” were propelled by a paddle
and oar. The paddler sat astern and steered and drove the
boat Indian-fashion. In the actual fishing the oar was not
used, the paddler having complete control of the boat, and
the other men attending to the net. The great drag net
swung between the boats and the lower extremity was kept
sweeping the bottom of the river by rounded stone weights.
The upper and lower net-lines were held by the linesman in
each boat, and as soon as anything touched the net his in-
variable cry of “thogay™ (take it) rang out, the under side of
the net was swiftly drawn up, the boats brought alongside
each other, and the fish caught as in a sack without chance of
escape.

The first “crew ” got to business quickly, and the men
were soon out in the middle of the stream. The second con-
sisted of the Captain, Peter O’Hara, the great “ Screecb-man,”
Meehal-na-Gamawn, and Malachy Gilmartin, the young wood-
ranger, whose knowledge of the river was wonderful, and who
could make any given point even on the darkest night. These
followed in more leisurely fashion.

It was a glorious night, indeed, upon the broad bosom of the
Suir. The Comeraghs, Mount Bolton, and Curraghmore, on
the County Waterford side, loomed gigantic over the flood,
and the yellow moonlight bathed summit, and field, and wood
with an effect like fairyland. On the Kilkenny bank the
scattered villagesgleamed whiteamid the trees, and the fissured
battlements of Corlody Castle flung black bars of shade athwart
the eddying depths beneath.

The poetry of the scene fell like balm on the soul of
Dermot Roche.

Kecping time with easy but powerful sweeps of the paddle.

22 THE GAELS OF MOONDHARRIG.

he began to sing in a low, sweet voice, a haunting old Gaelic
melody to which he had adapted these lines :—

“ THE SWEET RIVER SUIR,”

From Devil’s Bit to Thurles, from Golden unto Cahir,
By castle-crowned Ard-Finan running pure;

Past Carrick and Kilsheelan, ever sparkling, ever wheeling,
Flow the waters of the Sweet River Suir.

The Galtees and Slieve-Ardagh send their torrents to its flood,
Bright-Anner comes from storied Slieve-na-mon ;

The sunshine and the shadows follow fast across the meadows,
Till the dews o’ the morn are gone.

By rich, flowery fields of the pleasant Golden Vale
By the broken Norman tow’r and hamlet white,

The Jaughing of the Suir, man or maiden would allure,
When its glad waters dance in the light.

The winds sough and sob thro’ ruined abbey walls,
‘Weird music echoes from the fairy-mound ;

And sad, mystic rhymes of long-forgotten times,
In the crooning of the Suir resound.

In cool sheltered glens, where glossy hazels nod,
The wild linnet pipes a happy lay ;

Blithe thrush and blackbird singing, joyous melodies are flinging,
Thro’ brier-scented groves all day.

"Tis there that I'd dwell, for my heart is ever there,
‘Where Ormonde and wide Ossory stretch out ;

Where the rival Gaels are dashing and eager hurlers clashing,
Make din above the throng’s great shout.

I know a sunny meadow, gently sloping to the tide,
‘Where clover-blossoms welcome the wild bee ;

By a castle old and hoary, there I'd dream of Erin's glory,
While the hushed waters ran to the sea.

Ah, dear to me Killarney, where God’s smile is ever seen,
And fair thy leafy woodlands, Glenmalure !

But when this life is ended, and cold earth with earth is blended,
Let me rest by the Sweet River Suir.

As the fishers glided onward with the tide, the river
became narrower and deeper and the current more powerful,

WHEN NIGHT IS ON THE SUIR. 23

while the thick woods of Moonveen and Dongroove, on either
side, seemed to close in upon them, and a heavy shadow fell
over the water.

One of Meehal-na-Gamawn’s greatest weaknesses was the
supernatuml —

« Here is where the two Kenny boys were drowned one
wild night, goin’ over to cut hurleys,” said he in an awe-stricken
whisper; “it’s so deep here they were never found. Tom
Purcell an’ meself saw their faces under the water one moon-
light night we were fishin’ here—God between us an’ all harm.”

« Drock crioch ort a Mhicil” growled the woodranger,
« stick to your work, man, and don’t mind rameis.”

“ Thogay!” burst out Meehal, as sudden as a gunshot!
A vigorous push from the Captain and the other paddler sent
the boats close in, almost side by side, in a twinkling. The
net, violently agitated by a big, shining fish, was soon taken
into one of the cots. Before extricating his prey, Meehal-na-
Gamawn ended its struggles by a few blows on the head with
a short, heavy cudgel.

Deftly and quickly he released the big salmon, and sliding
it behind him, began throwing out the net once more.

“ What hurry are you in, Meehal ?”” said Dermot Roche ;
“let me have a look at the fish.”

Grumbling, Meehal hauled out the salmon to view.

“It’s easily seen, Dermot, boy,” he said, “ that you’re only
a fair-weather fisherman an’ not dependin’ on it for a livin’,—
lookin’ at a fish an’ maybe another runnin’ past us this minnit.”

BOHER-NA-GEEHA, which means the ““Road of the Winds,” is

THE MARCH TO BOHER-NA-GEEHA.

NEXT morning the sun rose into a wild and threatening sky,
and ere long his rays were shut in by thick, rolling banks of
vapour. The wind, as the day advanced, became stronger
and fiercer, until its roar drowned every other sound, and the
people began to look anxiously at the big stacks of corn in the
haggards, and the thatched roofs of their houses. Suddenly,
from the Moondharrig police barrack, emerged a body of about
twenty men. Each had a loaded rifle on his shoulder, and, to
meet contingencies, a heavy rain cloak. Inthe square formed
by the police as they marched, were three bailiffs and a
sherift’s officer. The three bailiffs, truculent-looking ruffians,
the scum of some large city, carried heavy sledge-hammers
beneath their coats. ~ Without doubt, in starting out so
suddenly on such a day, the “Crowbar Brigade,” as such
a party was called, expected to escape without notice by the
people. But the latter had been forewarned, and were on the
alert.

Scarcely had the hated eviction party sallied from the
barracks, when the chapel bell began to ring the dread alarm,
horn-calls reverberated among the hills, and crowds of people
thronged from every side. They followed up the “Crowbar
Brigade,” hemming them in in front and rear. Fierce impre-
cations and threats were hurled at the bailiffs, who were the

48 
THE GAELS OF MOONDHARRIG. 
special objects of the people's hatred, and the guarding police 
were hissed and groaned full heartily. Everything that could 
be done short of actual violence was done to delay the evicting 
party. Once or twice the stalwart peasants, armed with heavy 
blackthorns, pushed in so close that the Chief of Police gave 
orders to his men to get ready to fire, and the crowds fell 
back for the moment. At these times the cries of "Carrick-
shock '' rang out with a wild fierceness that caused the young 
Chief to become pale, and the men grasp their weapons 
nervously. For the dreadful affray at Carrickshock, where a 
strong body of police had been set upon and killed to a man, 
during the" Tithe War," had happened but a few miles away, 
and the same passionate and impulsive people were there 
to-day. 
It was a perilous and trying march for the evicting force. 
The wretched bailiffs were trembling with fear, and the police 
were wishing themselves well rid of their dangerous task. For 
an hour and a half they marched thus, the crowd swelling to 
enormous proportions, and growing more vociferous and angry 
at every step. 
They had now come to the foot of the hill on whose 
shoulder the little village of Boher-na-geeha was perched. The 
police looked up, and saw the "road of the winds" winding 
up into the mist and clouds. To-day the place amply justified 
its name. 
The storm, bowling like a thousand demons, swept 
down against them with much force, and they had often to 
turn their backs to it, in order to draw a long breath. It was 
"' toilsome and trying work, and the screaming of the wind, com-
bined with the angry shouts of the people, had such an effect 
on the police and bailiffs that their nerves were utterly shaken 
ere the ascent was half accomplished. 
A little more than half way upon the 1' road of thl:3 winds" 
the narrow passageway skirted the hillside horizontally for 11 
short distance, and was perfectly level. 
On the high side of 
the road the hill rose almost perpendicularly, and on the valley 
side was a quagmire or shaky-bog, as the people called it. A 
most treacherous place was this shaky-bog, looking green and 
firm to the eye, but many a poor animal, tempted by its ver-
dure, bad been drawn slowly beneitth the slimy ooze, and the

counh-y people had a sort of terror for the place, which was well 
borne out by its reputation. When the evicting party reached 
the level of the road, every man drew a long breath of relief. 
The towering cliffs were a shelter from the terrific wind, and 
they had time to look about them. 
Their nerve-racking ex-
perience up to this had not helped their spirits much, and the 
strange fact that when they looked behind them now, they 
saw the people were no longer following them, but were

The place about them look positively uncanny. They 
were Irish, like the people, and their vivid imaginations were 
working strongly. 
It was a place where the banshee or the 
pooka 'might dwell-a wild and dreary place that began to 
look unreal and enchanted to their eyes. 
This effect was in-
tensified when, on rounding the shoulder of the cliff, a tall 
Round Tower, with an ancient Celtic cross at its base, seemed 
to spring out of the mist, as if by magic. 
At this point the roadway suddenly deflectE:d, and went 
straight up the hill between two steep banks. This particular 
spot had a name of its own, and was called Bearna-na-geeha, 
or the '' Gap of the Winds," and to-day this was shown to be 
no misnomer. With a tremendous roar the storm swept down 
through the gap, and the evicting party stood for a moment, 
daunted by this new difficulty. 
At the same instant a great 
stone, as if detached by the force of the wind, came rumbling 
down the gap. As the police leaped aside to avoid it, they 
heard, above the storm, a shout of many voices, wild and 
threatening, that seemed to them the cry of the mountain-
spirits, whose realms they were invading. 
Scarcely had the 
unearthly yell died away, when another boulder, larger by 
many times than the first, came thundering down on the dis-
mayed and disorganized evicting force. 
The police cowered 
under the shelter of the bank as this fearful _missile lurched by, 
and, at the same time, another demoniac yell sounded in their 
ears! 
So unnerving were these strange happenings that the 
boldest of the men were pale with fright. 
Little more was 
needed to complete their panic, and the end was at hand. As

nious treatment accorded to himself arose from the fact that
they were persuaded he was a spy in the employ of the Excise.
They did nou intend to take any chances, buthad planned
to take him along in their boats and leave him bound and
gagged, somewhere along the river banks.

When Meehal-na-Gamawn had told all that he knew, the
Captain instituted a sort of trial of the prisoners. Candles
were lighted, and, the Gaels being seated on casks in various
picturesque attitudes, the proceedings commenced.

Meehal was the complainant and chief witness. Circum-
stantial evidence corroborative of his charges was there in
plenty. The Black Smuggler was allowed to put in a defence,
but only begged to be allowed to depart in peace; in fact he
had already decided that part of the country was not a safe
place in which to operate his still and worm, and the “run”
which had been interrupted by the unexpected advent of
Meehal was intended to be the last.

After hearing and seeing the evidence, the jury, without
leaving their seats, found the quartet guilty of high crimes
and misdemeanours.

The Captain then passed sentence.

THE RESCUE OF MEEHAL-NA-GAMAWN. 77

Their intention of quitting that part of the country was
highly commended, but they were told if ever they were seen
there again, dire punishment would follow. Meanwhile, for
their bad treatment of Meehal, they themselves were to remain
gagged and bound for the space of a day and a night. They
were, at the expiration of this time, to be released, and allowed
to proceed on their course down river. No confiscation of
illicit liquor was to be made, as it might lead to excess in
drink, and the Moondharrig Gaels were expected to give an
example of sobriety and manliness to all the people of Ireland.

“ However,” continued the Captain, “we will draw on the
contents of one of your water casks tothe extent of one toast,
and while our friend Meehal is taking another drop of the
mountain dew to revive himself, I will ask you to drink to the
success and prosperity of Meehal-na-Gamawn and the Moond-
harrig Hurlers!

“Meehal-na-Gamawn and the Moondharrig Hurlers,”
shouted the Gaels in chorus, and the toast was duly honoured.

leave these four pretty birds to their reflections, I'll call upon

still keeping close beside them. The Moondharrig men
appreciated this companionship of their beloved river, recall-
ing the many happy days and nights they had hauled the net
upon its broad bosom.
They crossed the Anner, that classic stream immortalized
by the poet Kickham, of idolized memory :—
¢She lived beside the Anner,
At the foot of Slieve-na-mon,
A gentle, pleasant girl,
‘With mild eyes like the dawn !”

Dermot Roche was gazing out over mountain and valley, a
dreamy look in his grey-blue Irish eyes, and the sad music of
the little ballad stole over his soul like a spell. Here, then,
had Charles Kickham planned and dreamed, and wasted away
with love for Ireland! The fields were beautifully soft and
green, the streams crystalline; the dim and misty hills faded
away in the distance, dotted with white hamlets and shadowy
woods, suggestive of romance and legend.

The Captain had read and appreciated ‘ Knocnagow,”
that true and unrivalled picture of southern Irish peasant life.
All the sweet sadness and sobbing laughter of that heart-
moving tale were in his soul as he gazed now upon the scenes
it describes.

In one of those far away little villages whose white-
washed walls shone through the hazy distance, that sweet
saint, Norah Lahy, had faded away in the bloom of her youth,
“a blossom plucked for heaven.” - There the inimitable Barney
Brodherick had flourished and sported—on the strength of the
two shillings worth of dance he got from Callaghan, cutting
weird and fantastic capers as he “welted ” the floor at Ned
Brophy’s wedding. There, too, “ Matt the Thrasher,” the great
ploughman and athlete, the typical Tipperary man, had per-
formed his wondrous feat of defeating Captain French at the

THE ROAD TO CLONMEL. 91

hammer-throwing contest. On that occasion Matt Donovan
had debated with himself as to whether he ought to allow the
Captain to “have the majority ” or not, but the sight of the
little village in the distance determined him, and for its credit
eclipsed all records. Captain Roche’s cheeks tingled now as
he thought of another little village far away whose *credit”
depended on himself to-day.

Then the swelling sides of Slieve-na-mon caught his eye,
and he was in the dreamy pastagain. This mountain from his

‘earliest years had a charm and a fascination of its own for

Captain Roche.

Rising in solitary magnificence, dominating the Golden
Vale, and overlooking the sinuous curves of the winding Suir,
it was a most conspicuous object seen from the native village.

It was sufficiently remote for one of an imaginative tem-
perament to fancy it an enchanted mountain in the midst of
fairyland, and Slieve-na-mon in its varying moods lent itself
lovingly to the illusion. In the wonderful Irish atmosphere
what varied shades and tints it assumed—now azure blue, like
the vault of heaven, now deeper-hued like the pulsing ocean ;
anon dark threatening and cloud-veiled, and again fair-smiling
in the sun’s warm caresses, showing vesture of green field,
brown heather-belt and golden-crowning gorse. And ever was
it associated in his boyish mind with the shadowy past of
Erin! Not only wasit the chosen dwelling-place of the fairies,
but also, it was haunted by the mighty shades of prehistoric
heroes, of Cuchullin and Conall Cearnach, of Finn and Oisin
and Oscar!

The slow-moving mists upon its broad breast were the hosts
of the Fianna and the fear-compelling Red Branch, sweeping
forward in resistless battle array, and the reverberating
thunder-peal was the shock of the onset, and the rattle of
buckler and shield. The gray mountain looks down into
“Cashel of the Kings,” and what scenes of splendour and
warlike pomp it must have viewed in Erin’s “olden, golden
days ’—the mustering of the Dalcassian and Eugenian bat-
talions, the royal conclaves of the Mononian Kings, their
contests, their feasts and revelries, whereat a hundred harps
clashed in swelling diapason!

It had viewed the array of Brian of the Tribute, and his
matchless troops surging onward from Kincora to the red
havoc of Cluan-Tarbh! Later on, the turbulent Normans—
De Burgo of Clanricard, Butler of Ormond, and the haughty
Geraldines of Kildare marched and counter-marched, fought
doughty battles, and reduced proud fortresses on the broad
plains spreading below ; and “ Murrough the Burner” passed,
leaving behiud a black and desolate trail :—

I see the smoke of your conquest rise,

Lav Laidlir Abu!

The Captain’s reverie was broken by a shout of “Clonmel” as
the train slowed down, and the strains of a brass band became
audible. The hurlers were received with welcoming cheers,
and lining up behind the band marched out to the hurling
grounds.

Clonmel, the place to which, as readers of Kickham will
remember, Billy Heffernan brought the load of turf—* Clo’'mel,
the town with the cloud over it,” is prettily situated on the
Suir in the middle of a rich and smiling country. The
Moondharrig men had pleasant memories of it and of its
people, having previously visited it on many occasions. The
townsfolk for their part gazed on the hurlers with unfeigned
interest, as Moondharrig’s fame was well known to them, and
the spirited march past of the Gaels to the strains of “The
Wearing of the Green ” was a sight worth seeing.

A great square field outside the town had been prepared
for the occasion. Six or seven thousand people had assembled
to watch the contest. Green flags flaunted gaily from the
goal posts, and a good-humoured and orderly crowd cheered
the arriving teams. The towering physique of the “gallant
Tipperary ” boys, and the fresh bloom and grace of the cailins
were immediately evident. Of course, as everywhere else, the
“Aunt Sally” man was there, with stentorian voice, calling
upon all and sundry to take * three shots a penny” at the

THE ROAD TO CLONMEL. 93

empty bottles which he placed on a  cock-shot ” for that pur-
pose. The trick-o’-the-loop man was at large among the
farmers’ boys, and the man who put his head out of a barrel
and dared one to hit the head was ducking from some well-
aimed throws.

He certainly deserved all the pennies he got, for his head
was in imminent jeopardy every time he winked his eyelids,
and if he winked twice in succession he was lost, for the
“Tipps” are not called  stone-throwers ” for nothing.

The Munster picked hurlers were already on the ground
prepared for action. Tall, agile, wiry youths they were, look-
ing to have an unlimited supply of speed and endurance.
They were dallying a little with the ball by way of practice,
and their handling of the caman showed the unmistakable
touch of players brought up to it from eaxliest boyhood.

The Moondharrig men were not long in getting ready for
the fray.

Stripping off to their green jerseys, and tightening their
grip of the camans, they went into the field picking their
steps, like the light-footed “Kilkenny Cats” they were. A
great silence began to settle down on the crowds—the excite-
ment was spreading to them, and expectancy was upon every
face. The gaze of all was on the two famous teams now
approaching one another.

The Captain spoke & last word to his men in a low tone.
«“Now, boys, let us do our best. We cau beat them if we only
keep our heads—don’t mind the crowds, and keep your eye on
the ball. Have no fear of the Munster men—there are nomen
that can handle a caman better than the Moondharrig men.
Don’t forget that they’re all prayin’ for us at home, and let us
bring good news to the little village by the river "——

The referee’s whistle cut in sharp and clear and the
forty-two Gaels lined up for the All-Ireland Championship
Match.

The All-Ireland Championship.

THE forty-two Gaels (twenty-one toa side) were in two close
parallel lines in the centre of the grounds, each team facing
towards their opponents goal. Placed man on man, each
player took the end of his opponent’s caman in his hand, and
two officials elevating a rod over the hurler’s heads, counted
them off in couples.

The Gaels in the meantime were measuring each other
with their eyes. This was a long-looked for moment, as the
fame of the two teams had gone afar through the land. The
Moycullens, Munster champions, were the taller men, but were
not so heavy-framed as the stalwart Suirside ploughman, and
the former seemed more confident and more at home on
the field. :

The Moondharrig men never could get rid of a certain
anxiety and nervousness before and during the first few
minutes of a match, and with sharp opponents to take account
of this, notable advantages might be gained over them.
Captain Roche feared for this, but his dependance on the cul-
baire, Will Hanlon, a veteran between the posts, was strong
and well-founded. p

The goal-man was standing close beside the Captain, and
the latter admired the sang froid of his lieutenant.

Heartily slapping the powerful custodian of the posts upon
the back, Roche cried «“ M’anam asther hu—Hanlon, I wxs'h
they were all as solid as you, but save us till the boys’ blood is
up, an’ we'll leave you little to do after that.” y

“T1 do id, Captain, never foar,” and the grim cul baire
grinned reassuringly.

Then there was a cry of “ball in play” and a clash of
camans like a musket volley. The referee had suddenly rolled
the ball down the line of players; the backs flew to position,
and the match was on!

THE ALL-IRELAND CHAMPIONSHIP. 95

The ball was held at centre for some exciting seconds.
The on-looking crowds, hitherto silent, broke into a frenzy of
cries for the opposing teams— Moondharrig go bragh,” con-
flicted with cries of “Moycullen Abu” and “Munster to the
front—clear the way.”

The music of the camans was inspiring tolisten to. They
clicked and slashed ; the knot of players swayed and struggled,
then suddenly broke. The ball got loose and was swiftly
taken possession of by Munster. Like a whirlwind it was
swept towards Moondharrig’s goal. Then the Captain’s fears
were partly realised. The Kilkenny men tried to stop the
attack, but in their nervousness fumbled and missed one after
another. The ball zig-zagged a bit, then two swift stroke sent
it in like a bullet on the cul baire.

Had there been an ordinary goal-man between the posts
Munster would assuredly have scored, but Hanlon was the best
goal-man in the South, and was well on the alert. As the
sphere plunged in breast high he swung on it viciously
and with faultless aim. There was a short, sweet “click ” and
the leather soared back as if shot from a catapult!

Dermot Roche was angry with his men. They had gone
to pieces before that splendid Munster rush, and something
was needed to “get their blood up” or the game might
be lost. Now, there was a certain local term of reproach
which, when applied to the Suirside men, never failed to
arouse their anger and combativeness. Above the noises of the
field the stentorian voice of the Captain rang out—* Wake up,
Moondharrig! the Munster men are calling ye cabbage
eaters!”

The Moondharrig men pricked up their ears at the hate-
ful name—cabbage eaters,eh ? They would soon show them !”

The ball at this moment was in the possession of the
Moondharrig left wing, and the left wing flared up in a trice
like a swarm of angry bees. With short, lightning strokes,
playing beautiful combination, they took it down the field
towards the Moycullen goal. Their heavy centre rush, sweep-
ing everything from its way, bore down parallel with them, to
receive the ball when the wings thought well of giving it up.

96 THE GAELS OF MOONDHARRIG.

But the wings never gave up the ball until they surged
into the centre mass twenty yards in front of the goal.

Like victims to the car of Juggernaut the Munster backs
plunged in but sacrificed themselves in vain. Nothing c?uld
stop the Suirside ploughmen, and the ball was in their midst,
tenderly looked after, and adroitly manceuvred by that
« foxiest” of seriobs, Pete O’Hara. The Munster goal-keepex
made a frantic charge, but was brushed aside like a reed.
With a wild cheer that had rung before at Carrickshock,
on under the bar swept irresistible Moondharrig !

The goal umpire waved a coloured flag. « First goal for
Leinster,” he announced ! The Moondharrig men came out of
the melee flushed and smiling. The Moycullen veterans
picked up their camans, astonishment depicted on their faces.
It was long since they had got such another mauling, but
being old hands they took it good humouredly, their self-
confidence not being for an instant shaken.

Captain Roche was gloating to himself over the success of
his little ruse. His men, being now warmed up, might be
depended on to play the game of their lives and his fore-
bodings of disastrous defeat were driven away.

The Moycullen goal man now “pucked off” and both
teams settled down to work in earnest. It was a grand exhi-
bition of the national game—the game of Cuchullin and of
Finn, played with consummate skill and coolness with never
a loss of temper on either side. The ball travelled up and
down the field with electrical velocity for quite a long time.
The backs on both sides seemed invulnerable to attack and sent
the leather back into midfield on every occasion. Among the
younger players Moran Og O’Laughlin so distinguished him-
self by dashing and clever play as to win plaudits even from
the opposing crowd. Hartigan “Aulinn” had the satisfaction of
completely out-playing his cousin, Kiernan Brennan, de-
moralizing that boastful individual by a few severe body-
checks and cowing him so that he left his proper position and
went to play on another part of the field.

The Moycullen veterans excelled in ground strokes, lift-
ing the ball every time and mostly taking it on the run. The

THE ALL-IRELAND CHAMPIONSHIP. 97

Kilkenny men, using a shorter style of caman, were peculiarly
skilful in “raising ” and striking the ball in the air.

This last makes beautiful play for the spectators. The
players flashed up and down the field, running like wild deer,
and as they ran they struck at the ball bounding past, never
missing it once. Often, of course, two combatants met with a
sounding crash, sometimes breaking their strong camans.

The first half-hour of play was almost elapsed when the
Munster men worked & surprise on their opponents. “ Whip ”
Sheridan and Shanahan, their two famous screebs, against
whom the Captain had repeatedly warned his men, had been
a long time looking for their opportunity. Itcame at last, and
Sheridan got the ball. He struck to Shanahan and Shanahan
back to him. Dodging and twisting in the most bewildering
manner they succeeded in completely out-manceuvring the
Kilkenny backs. The cul-baire made a fine attempt to stop
them, and although the ball was shot on himat only five yards
distance he managed to meet it with his caman, but glancing
obliquely the leather went through for a goal a -second or two
before the whistle blew for the half time.

The Gaels, glad of a little rest, flung themselves down on
the sward wherever they happened to be. Here a green-
shirted Moondharrig man might be seen in the very midst of
a strong company of Moycullen’s, whose colours were “ black
and amber.” There at another spot the tables were turned,
and a Moycullen Gael found himself surrounded by a verdant
circle of brawny Kilkenny men. But they got along famously
together, and the sallies of Celtic wit that presently began to
fly left the Gaels in exceeding good humour with each other.

The Captain walked leisurely over to the side lines where
he saw his friend, Meehal-na-Gamawn, standing. He was soon
surrounded by a crowd, many of whom proceeded to give him
pointers as to how the game should be played and how it
could be won. The Captain listened to these knowing ones
with a good-humoured smile, but he was heard to mutter an
old Irish proverb under his breath—“¢s maith an t-iomanwidhe
am 'te bhiomn ar am gelaidh ” (the best hurler is ever on the
fence). The pride that shone in the eyes of Meehal-na-Gamawn,
as he welcomed the Captain, was something worth seeing.

The Burial of Moran Og. 
It was a dreary November day in Moondharrig. A cold mist 
swept from shrouded Slieve-na-mon across the valley, and hid 
the surrounding mountains from view. 
Rivulets flowed along 
the sides of the roads, and small ponds were to be seen cover-
ing the fields. 
It was indeed a day for comfort by the blazing 
hearth, but all Moondharrig was abroad. 
Along the road

had taken part in two big matches and won great glory to

The hearts of the Gaels were heavy in their breast

for a tragic touch was added to the affair by the fact that 
Moran Og had just spent some months in prison for'' resisting

to the brutal treatment he had received while serving his

The cause of the young man's incarceration was 
this: the " Crow-bar Brigade" as the evicting posse was 
at work evicting a family, and during the proceedings one 
of the bailiffs used vile language towards, and brutally struck 
a young girl, who wild with grief had clung to the door-post 
as she was being ejected. _ The next instant a powerful youth 
burst through the cordon of police, reckless of their bayonets, 
and with a terrible blow of his clenched hand stretched the 
ruffian insensible in his tracks. For this offence '' obstructing 
Her Majesty's forces," Moran Og for it was he, was sentenced to 
three mouths imprisonment with hard labour. It was little 
wonder that the people who followed his corpse to its last

"God save ye, boys," saluted the priest. "I suppose it is on a.

"You did the right thing," said the priest, "the journey 
will be simply a pleasure with two such sturdy companions. 
Here, Malachy, take this lantern and bring my horse from the

It was not long before the three horsemen were on their 
road, setting face in the direction of the mountains where the 
sick man lived. 
As they climbed to higher ground the wind 
became immeasurably stronger, and the rain beat against them 
mercilessly. 
The Gaels kept a strict silence knowing that the 
priest was carrying the Blessed Sacrament, but even if they 
were permitted, conversation would have been impossible in 
the shrill tumult that seemed to fill the world: At the pass of 
Barna-na-Geeha, or the "Gap of the Winds," the impetuosity 
of the gale grew to be something tremendous. 
The horsemen 
could no longer sit erect in their saddles but had to bend 
behind their horse's herds to escape the feeling of suffocation. 
With the howl of ten thousand demons the hurricane 
swept through the narrow defile, often bringing the horses to 
f1 standstill and making them stagger in their tracks. 
To the priest, intent on his supernatural errand, it seemed 
:1s though diabolical force was being used against them to 
hamper and delay. 
Irresistibly there came into his mind II weird legend cur-
rent in that part of the country, and the scene of which they 
had just passed. 
A priest, said the legend, had been called 
hastily in the night to a~tend a dying p~rson. 
He imme-
diately hurried on his way, and m:ide good progress as far as 
a certain ruined castle that stood. by the way-side.

priest drew nigh to this ancient fortress, dim and solemn in

Presently a voice of super-

Such was the magic and charm of that wonderful voice 
that the poor priest forgot his pressing and sacred duty and 
stood like one in a trance until the singing ceased. 
Then the 
spell being broken he stepped forward a few paces and looked 
inside the ruin. 
To his horror there was no human being there, but in a 
shaft of moonlight on the grass-grown floor sat a great wolf-
hound, that glared at him with hateful eyes of fil'e ! 
With a shock of anguish and shame the priest realised 
that he had been delayed on his sacred errand by a ruse of the 
evil one. 
Hr,stening forward he endeavoured to make up for lost 
time, but to no purpose, for when he arrived at the house of 
the man to whom be had been called he found him already 
dead! The atonement made by the priest of the legend for 
this momentary carelessness w11s an after 0life of rigorous 
penance and austerity. 
Father Dermody prayed that the hidden God reposing 
now at his breast might save him always from the sorrow of 
being too late to comfort a poor soul in agony and need. 
His 
companions, too, were praying, and the Divine presence brought 
comfort and assurance to their hearts. 
And now as they reached the summit of the "Gap of the 
Winds," where had the storm continued in unabated fury 
there would have been danger of being blown to destruction 
over the high cliffs that here impended, suddenly came a 
strange and unaccountable lull. 
This continued until the party descended from the ex-
posed ridge into the skelter of the bulging mountain side. 
The worst of the road was now behind them, . and with grate-
ful hearts they urged their horses to a gallop, nor drew reins 
till they brought up in front of the cottage of Meehal-na-
Gamawn. 
The mother of the latter met them at the door, and 
there was a relieved and thankful look on her face as she sa IV'

Placing the Blessed Sacrament on a small table on which

At a glance the priest could see that the shadow of

again see the light of day in this vale of tears. 
Putting on his stole, the priest heard the dying man's 
confession. 
Then calling in the mother and the two Gaels he 
bade them kneel down while he administered the Holy 
Via tic um. 
It was a solemn and peaceful scene in that little chamber, 
while outside the heavy din of the storm rose and fell. 
Tears flowed fast down the manly cheeks of the Gaels as 
they saw the priest bending to give the last Communion, 
while Meehal-na-Gamawn, their beloved friend, welcomed his 
God with the most tender sentiments of love and hope. How 
small and trivial and foolish appeared 2.11 earthly struggles 
and jealousies and triumphs in the presence of this awful 
hour of death! They struck their breasts and prayed for 
pardon for their sins, and that a holy and well-prepared-for 
death might also be their happy portion at the end of their 
lives. 
Ah ! well indeed had they loved their dying friend, 
9,nd could it be possible, even now, they would gladly die in 
his place! 
And the noble Irish mother knelt bravely there with a 
halo of sainthood about her sweet face, and like many another 
strong-hearted Irish mother offers her son to God without 
reserve. Then the priest performed the rast solemn anointing, 
and gave the blessing in exfrernis to further strengthen that 
soul that was now girding itself for the final ordeal. 
An anxious look came into the eyes of Meehs,l-na-Gamawn.

Slowly the night hours dragged past, and the dying man

From his incoherent sentences 
they could gather that he was wandering once again with his 
friends, the Gaels. 
Once again he watched them run and 
strike the swift-bounding ball. 
With strange thrills the Captain and Malachy heard him 
call out their names-now in tones of entreaty, anon in tones 
of pride and commendation, as though they had just performed 
some wondrous feat. 
The priest, closely observant, noted a great change coming 
slowly over the features of the dying man. 
"We will recite the last litanies now, in the name of 
God," he said. 
Putting the crucifix in the hand of Meehal-na-Gamawn 
the priest knelt, and began the " Recommendation of a Soui

As he did so, consciousness returned for a few moments

1'0Sponses. 
"Lord have ~ercy on me," he prayed. "Christ 
have mercy on me-Holy Mary pray for me," then once again

The day of the famous match between Moondharrig and 
Tullagmore arrived at last. 
Great :vas the concourse that gathered from far and near 
to see the rival teams and hear the clashing camans. 
And deep was the suspense of the crowds, for until the 
last few moments the issue of the contest was in doubt. 
Five minutes before the referee's whistle called off th1 
gam~ the teams were even, at two goals each. 
Moondharrig, 
fightmg desperfl'.tely around their own goal posts, expected 
any moment their opponents would score and win the game. 
Heavy was the depression and r.nxiety prevailing amongst the

The Captain’s version of the affair, as told to Fathex

«The fight around the Moondharrig goal had been a
terrible one,” said the Captain, “an’ I was nearly played ont
when I got that chance for a run. However, I did the best I
could, an’ took the ball up field. When I got near their goal
the strain grew too much, I weakened an’ a mist came before
my eyes. Then in an instant—an’ "twas his prayers that did
it, father—my sight cleared, an’ I saw Meehal-na-Gamawn &s
plain as day standing at the end-line encouraging me with
the very look of his eyes like he used to do. It never struck
me that he was dead, or that 'twas strange for him to be there,
but I got great courage an’ the strength of three men. 'Twas
casy scorin’ a goal after that. But, father,” continued the
Captain, with heaving breast, 1 miss the poor fellow texrible,
0 T do—God rest his soul in peace this day.” And Father
